summary:
The Golden Horde (c. 1242–1502 AD) was the northwestern khanate of the fragmented [[event:91|Mongol Empire]], established by Batu Khan following the [[event:2227|invasion of Eastern Europe]], controlling the Pontic-Caspian steppe from the Carpathians to the Kazakh steppe and exerting suzerainty over the Russian principalities for over two centuries — the 'Tatar Yoke' that shaped Russian political culture, national memory, and development in ways that remain debated by historians to the present day. The Golden Horde rulers converted to Islam in the early 14th century under Khan Özbeg, becoming a Muslim state while maintaining authority over Orthodox Christian Russian subjects.

The Russian principalities (Moscow, Tver, Novgorod, Vladimir) paid tribute to the Golden Horde, sent their princes to seek approval (yarlik) for their rule at the Horde's capital Sarai on the Volga, and provided military contingents when required. The experience of Mongol rule — with its ruthless taxation, demands for submission, and periodic punitive raids on cities that resisted — profoundly influenced Russian political development: some historians argue it contributed to the development of Russia's autocratic political tradition, though others dispute the extent of Mongol institutional influence. The rise of Moscow as the dominant Russian principality owed partly to its willingness to cooperate with the Horde as tax-collector and intermediary. The Golden Horde began fragmenting in the late 14th century, with [[event:2276|Timur's]] raids delivering devastating blows; Russian princes under Dmitri Donskoi defeated a Horde army at the [[event:3034|Battle of Kulikovo]] (1380), and the Horde dissolved entirely by the early 16th century.
